1→//! Shared helpers for OpenAI-compatible streaming with tools.
     2→//!
     3→//! This module provides reusable functions for parsing OpenAI-style SSE streams
     4→//! that include tool calls. Used by openai.rs, github_copilot.rs, and kimi_code.rs.
     5→
     6→use std::collections::{HashMap, HashSet};
     7→
     8→use {
     9→    moltis_agents::model::{
    10→        ChatMessage, CompletionResponse, StreamEvent, ToolCall, Usage, UserContent,
    11→        decode_tool_call_arguments,
    12→    },
    13→    serde::Serialize,
    14→    tracing::trace,
    15→};
    16→
    17→use super::{
    18→    schema_normalization::sanitize_schema_for_openai_compat,
    19→    strict_mode::patch_schema_for_strict_mode,
    20→};
    21→
    22→// ============================================================================
    23→// OpenAI Tool Schema Types
    24→// ============================================================================
    25→// These types enforce the correct structure for OpenAI-compatible APIs.
    26→// Using typed structs instead of manual JSON prevents missing fields at compile time.
    27→//
    28→// References:
    29→// - Chat Completions: https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/function-calling
    30→// - Responses API: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-foundry/openai/how-to/responses
    31→// ============================================================================
    32→
    33→/// Chat Completions API tool format (nested under "function").
    34→///
    35→/// ```json
    36→/// { "type": "function", "function": { "name": "...", ... } }
    37→/// ```
    38→#[derive(Debug, Serialize)]
    39→pub struct ChatCompletionsTool {
    40→    #[serde(rename = "type")]
    41→    pub tool_type: &'static str,
    42→    pub function: ChatCompletionsFunction,
    43→}
    44→
    45→/// The function definition nested inside ChatCompletionsTool.
    46→#[derive(Debug, Serialize)]
    47→pub struct ChatCompletionsFunction {
    48→    pub name: String,
    49→    pub description: String,
    50→    pub parameters: serde_json::Value,
    51→    pub strict: bool,
    52→}
    53→
    54→/// Responses API tool format (flat, name at top level).
    55→///
    56→/// ```json
    57→/// { "type": "function", "name": "...", "parameters": {...}, "strict": true }
    58→/// ```
    59→#[derive(Debug, Serialize)]
    60→pub struct ResponsesApiTool {
    61→    #[serde(rename = "type")]
    62→    pub tool_type: &'static str,
    63→    pub name: String,
    64→    pub description: String,
    65→    pub parameters: serde_json::Value,
    66→    pub strict: bool,
    67→}
    68→
    69→/// Convert tool schemas to OpenAI Chat Completions function-calling format.
    70→///
    71→/// Uses the nested `function` object format required by Chat Completions API:
    72→/// ```json
    73→/// { "type": "function", "function": { "name": "...", ... } }
    74→/// ```
    75→///
    76→/// When `strict` is `true`, patches schemas for strict mode compliance:
    77→/// - `additionalProperties: false` on all object schemas
    78→/// - All properties included in `required` array
    79→/// - Originally-optional properties made nullable via array-form types
    80→///
    81→/// When `strict` is `false`, schemas are sanitized but not patched for strict
    82→/// mode. This avoids array-form types like `["boolean", "null"]` that
    83→/// non-OpenAI backends (e.g. Google via OpenRouter) reject.
    84→///
    85→/// See: <https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/function-calling>
    86→pub fn to_openai_tools(tools: &[serde_json::Value], strict: bool) -> Vec<serde_json::Value> {
    87→    let result: Vec<serde_json::Value> = tools
    88→        .iter()
    89→        .filter_map(|t| {
    90→            let mut params = t["parameters"].clone();
    91→            sanitize_schema_for_openai_compat(&mut params);
    92→            if strict {
    93→                patch_schema_for_strict_mode(&mut params);
    94→            }
    95→
    96→            let name = t["name"].as_str()?.to_string();
    97→            let description = t["description"].as_str().unwrap_or("").to_string();
    98→
    99→            // Use typed struct to ensure all required fields are present
   100→            let tool = ChatCompletionsTool {
   101→                tool_type: "function",
   102→                function: ChatCompletionsFunction {
   103→                    name: name.clone(),
   104→                    description,
   105→                    parameters: params,
   106→                    strict,
   107→                },
   108→            };
   109→
   110→            trace!(tool_name = %name, strict, "converted tool to Chat Completions format");
   111→
   112→            // Serialize to Value for compatibility with existing API
   113→            serde_json::to_value(tool).ok()
   114→        })
   115→        .collect();
   116→
   117→    trace!(tools_count = result.len(), "to_openai_tools complete");
   118→    result
   119→}
   120→
   121→/// Convert tool schemas to OpenAI Responses API function-calling format.
   122→///
   123→/// Uses the flat format required by the Responses API where `name` is at top level:
   124→/// ```json
   125→/// { "type": "function", "name": "...", "parameters": {...}, "strict": true }
   126→/// ```
   127→///
   128→/// This is the format used by OpenAI Codex and the Responses API.
   129→///
   130→/// Patches schemas for strict mode compliance:
   131→/// - `additionalProperties: false` on all object schemas
   132→/// - All properties included in `required` array
   133→///
   134→/// See: <https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-foundry/openai/how-to/responses>
   135→pub fn to_responses_api_tools(tools: &[serde_json::Value]) -> Vec<serde_json::Value> {
   136→    let result: Vec<serde_json::Value> = tools
   137→        .iter()
   138→        .filter_map(|t| {
   139→            // Clone parameters and patch for strict mode
   140→            let mut params = t["parameters"].clone();
   141→            sanitize_schema_for_openai_compat(&mut params);
   142→            patch_schema_for_strict_mode(&mut params);
   143→
   144→            let name = t["name"].as_str()?.to_string();

Perfect! I have all the information I need. Let me create a comprehensive summary of the findings:

## Summary: Moltis Reasoning Model Detection and Handling

I've completed a thorough exploration of how Moltis detects and handles reasoning-capable models. Here are the key findings:

### 1. **Reasoning Model Detection** (`/crates/providers/src/model_capabilities.rs`)

The system determines if a model supports reasoning through a hardcoded pattern-matching function `supports_reasoning_for_model()` (lines 226-257):

**Supported Models:**
- **Anthropic**: Claude Opus 4.5+, Claude Sonnet 4.5+, Claude Opus 4.6+, Claude Sonnet 4.6+, Claude 3.7 Sonnet
- **OpenAI**: o1, o1-mini, o3, o3-mini, o4, o4-mini, GPT-5.x series
- **Google Gemini**: Gemini 2.5+ and Gemini 3 (with thinking)
- **DeepSeek**: DeepSeek R1, deepseek-reasoner

The logic is simple prefix matching: `id.starts_with("claude-opus-4-5")`, etc.

### 2. **Reasoning Effort Levels** (`/crates/config/src/schema.rs`)

The `ReasoningEffort` enum has three levels:
- `Low` - minimal reasoning
- `Medium` - balanced reasoning
- `High` - deep reasoning

```rust
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, Serialize, Deserialize)]
#[serde(rename_all = "lowercase")]
pub enum ReasoningEffort {
    Low,
    Medium,
    High,
}
```

### 3. **Model ID Reasoning Suffix Parsing** (`/crates/providers/src/model_id.rs`)

Model IDs can have an optional `@reasoning-{level}` suffix appended:
- Format: `<model-id>@reasoning-low`, `<model-id>@reasoning-medium`, `<model-id>@reasoning-high`
- Example: `"anthropic::claude-opus-4-5@reasoning-high"` → parsed as base ID with `High` reasoning effort
- The `split_reasoning_suffix()` function (lines 36-47) extracts the base model ID and effort level

### 4. **Model Capabilities Storage** (`/crates/providers/src/model_capabilities.rs`)

The `ModelCapabilities` struct (lines 264-272) includes a `reasoning: bool` field that indicates if a model supports extended thinking. This is inferred during model registration using the `infer()` method.

### 5. **OpenAI Provider Reasoning Implementation** (`/crates/providers/src/openai/provider/core.rs`)

OpenAI providers store `reasoning_effort: Option<ReasoningEffort>` and apply it differently depending on the API endpoint:

**Chat Completions API** (lines 104-108):
```rust
fn apply_reasoning_effort_chat(&self, body: &mut serde_json::Value) {
    if let Some(effort) = self.reasoning_effort_str() {
        body["reasoning_effort"] = serde_json::json!(effort);  // top-level string
    }
}
```

**Responses API** (lines 114-118):
```rust
fn apply_reasoning_effort_responses(&self, body: &mut serde_json::Value) {
    if let Some(effort) = self.reasoning_effort_str() {
        body["reasoning"] = serde_json::json!({ "effort": effort });  // nested object
    }
}
```

The effort is converted to string: `Low` → `"low"`, `Medium` → `"medium"`, `High` → `"high"`

### 6. **Anthropic Provider Reasoning Implementation** (`/crates/providers/src/anthropic.rs`)

Anthropic uses `thinking` configuration with `budget_tokens` (lines 69-91):

```rust
fn apply_thinking(&self, body: &mut serde_json::Value) {
    let budget_tokens: u64 = match effort {
        ReasoningEffort::Low => 4096,
        ReasoningEffort::Medium => 10240,
        ReasoningEffort::High => 32768,
    };
    body["thinking"] = serde_json::json!({
        "type": "enabled",
        "budget_tokens": budget_tokens,
    });
}
```

Automatically bumps `max_tokens` if it's less than or equal to `budget_tokens` (to comply with API requirements).

### 7. **Model Provider Trait Methods** (`/crates/agents/src/model.rs`)

The `LlmProvider` trait has two reasoning-related methods:
- `fn reasoning_effort(&self) -> Option<ReasoningEffort>` - returns the currently configured effort
- `fn with_reasoning_effort(self: Arc<Self>, effort: ReasoningEffort) -> Option<Arc<dyn LlmProvider>>` - returns a new provider instance with the effort set (returns `None` if unsupported)

### 8. **Reasoning Variant Registration** (`/crates/providers/src/registry/registration.rs`)

The registry automatically creates virtual model variants for reasoning-capable models (lines 1078-1100):

- **Base models** are registered as-is
- **For each reasoning-capable model**, three additional entries are created:
  - `{model_id}@reasoning-low` 
  - `{model_id}@reasoning-medium`
  - `{model_id}@reasoning-high`
- These virtual IDs are resolved back to the base provider with reasoning effort applied via `get()` (lines 1022-1042)
- Display names are augmented: `"Claude Opus 4.5 (high reasoning)"`

### 9. **Agent Preset Configuration** (`/crates/config/src/schema/agents.rs`)

Agent presets can specify `reasoning_effort: Option<ReasoningEffort>` (line 151), which is applied to sub-agents spawned via the `spawn_agent` tool.

### 10. **Reasoning Effort Application Flow** (`/crates/tools/src/spawn_agent.rs`)

When spawning a sub-agent:
1. Resolve the provider from registry
2. Check if the agent preset has `reasoning_effort` configured
3. Call `provider.with_reasoning_effort(effort)` to create a new provider with that effort
4. Warn if the preset overrides an effort already in the model ID suffix (lines 141-175)

### Key Files Summary

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `/crates/providers/src/model_capabilities.rs` | `supports_reasoning_for_model()` pattern matching, `ModelCapabilities::reasoning` bool |
| `/crates/providers/src/model_id.rs` | `split_reasoning_suffix()` parsing, `@reasoning-{level}` suffix constants |
| `/crates/providers/src/openai/provider/core.rs` | OpenAI reasoning request body construction |
| `/crates/providers/src/anthropic.rs` | Anthropic thinking configuration (`budget_tokens`) |
| `/crates/agents/src/model.rs` | `LlmProvider` trait reasoning methods |
| `/crates/providers/src/registry/registration.rs` | Virtual reasoning variant registration and resolution |
| `/crates/config/src/schema/agents.rs` | `AgentPreset.reasoning_effort` configuration |
| `/crates/tools/src/spawn_agent.rs` | Preset reasoning effort application |

### Important Note on OpenRouter and Grok

Grok models would be handled as OpenAI-compatible models through OpenRouter. Since OpenRouter is a proxy that passes through to underlying providers, the reasoning detection would still work based on the model name pattern (e.g., `grok-4.20` wouldn't match any reasoning patterns and would be treated as non-reasoning unless explicitly marked as such). OpenRouter would proxy the reasoning effort parameters to the underlying provider.
